title: Joins & Shuffles Recognition Track — Junior
description: A 10-exercise junior recognition track. Read PySpark join code: predict outputs, spot the difference, find bugs that run without errors, and review production-style pipelines. Five modules: PJSJ1 Predicting Output · PJSJ2 Join Types & Nulls · PJSJ3 Column Ambiguity · PJSJ4 Shuffle vs Broadcast · PJSJ5 Reading Real Code. Companion to PS7 (joins theory).

datasets.description: Shared NordGrid meter readings (20 rows). Same sample as the JA aggregations track — the joins-shuffles material reuses the engineered null distributions and substation-id coverage.
Substations reference (6 rows). Carries a region column with the same name as meters — exercises column-collision scenarios.

tasks.title: What Does This Inner Join Return?

description.context: You are reviewing a teammate's pull request that joins NordGrid's 20-row meter sample to the 6-row substations dimension. They want the result to feed into a downstream regional report. Before approving, you want to predict the row count of the join result without running it.
description.task: How many rows does `result` contain after this join?

evidence.title: Input sample
evidence.content:
meters has 20 rows. Every substation_id in meters has a matching row in substations. substations has 6 rows total — one per substation_id appearing in meters.

Meters Columns: meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type, substation_id

Substations Columns: substation_id, name, region, capacity_mw

Substation Distribution In Meters:
  S-N-01: 4
  S-N-02: 4
  S-S-01: 3
  S-S-02: 3
  S-E-01: 4
  S-W-01: 2

evidence.label: snippet.py
evidence.content:
meters = spark.read.csv('datasets/meters.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)
subs = spark.read.csv('datasets/substations.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)

result = meters.join(subs, on='substation_id', how='inner')

fields.label: How many rows does `result` contain after this join?
options: 20 rows — every meter row finds exactly one substation match in the dimension
26 rows — the meter rows plus the 6 substation rows are concatenated together
120 rows — 20 meter rows times 6 substation rows from the Cartesian product
6 rows — one per distinct substation_id, since join collapses to unique keys
fields.correctAnswer: 20 rows — every meter row finds exactly one substation match in the dimension
fields.rationale: An inner join keeps each left-side row once for every match it finds on the right. Each meter has exactly one substation_id, and each substation_id appears exactly once in the substations dimension. So every meter row gets one match, and the result has the same row count as the left side: 20. If the substations table had duplicate substation_id values (e.g., two rows for 'S-N-01'), the result would have more than 20 rows — that is the fan-out case covered in PJSJ1.4.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option B: Treats join as a union — adding the row counts of both sides. Joins multiply matches; they do not concatenate rows.

Option C: Confuses inner join with cross join. Cross join produces every left-right pair (the Cartesian product); inner join only pairs rows where the join key matches.

Option D: Confuses join with groupBy. The result row count is driven by the left side's row count and the right side's match count, not by the distinct values of the join key.

tasks.ps7Reference: PS7 lesson 2 (Inner Joins) — establishes that inner join keeps each left row once per right-side match.

tasks.title: The Join That Returned More Rows Than Expected

description.context: A teammate ships a daily regional report. Their pipeline reads 20 meter rows, joins to a substations dimension, and produces output. The next morning the report shows 26 rows where 20 were expected. The pipeline ran without errors and the output looks plausible — but the row count is wrong. You need to find the bug.
description.task: What is the bug, and what is the production consequence?

evidence.title: Input sample
evidence.content:
Substations dimension was loaded from a source that emitted some substations twice (different timestamps, same substation_id). Substation S-N-01 appears twice; substation S-S-02 appears three times; the rest appear once each. Total substations rows: 6 unique IDs, 9 physical rows.

Meters Columns: meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type, substation_id

Substations With Dups: S-N-01: 2 rows, S-S-02: 3 rows, S-N-02 / S-S-01 / S-E-01 / S-W-01: 1 row each (9 total)

Meter Rows Using S N 01: 4

Meter Rows Using S S 02: 3

evidence.label: snippet.py
evidence.content:
meters = spark.read.csv('datasets/meters.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)
subs = spark.read.csv('datasets/substations.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)

result = meters.join(subs, on='substation_id', how='left')
print(f'meters rows: {meters.count()}')   # prints 20
print(f'result rows: {result.count()}')   # prints 26

# The downstream report computes per-substation totals from `result`.
# The totals are inflated and Finance flagged the report at end-of-day.

fields.label: What is the bug, and what is the production consequence?
options: The bug is on the meters side — the meters table has duplicate (meter_id, date) rows that inflate the join input; deduplicating meters before the join would restore the expected row count of 20
The bug is on the substations side — the dimension has duplicate substation_id rows; the join multiplies meter rows by the match count, inflating per-substation totals
The bug is the join type — left join keeps right-side rows that have no matching meter, adding 6 unmatched rows to the output; switching to inner join would restore the expected row count of 20

The bug is the schema inference — Spark inferred substation_id as integer for one table and string for the other, creating type-coerced join matches that drop and add rows; specifying explicit schemas would fix it
fields.correctAnswer: The bug is on the substations side — the dimension has duplicate substation_id rows; the join multiplies meter rows by the match count, inflating per-substation totals
fields.rationale: Right-side fan-out is the bug — the dimension carries the same key on multiple rows, and the join multiplies. When meters.join(subs, ...) runs, every meter row matches every duplicate substation row on its key. S-N-01 has 2 substation rows and 4 meter rows → 8 rows. S-S-02 has 3 substation rows and 3 meter rows → 9 rows. The four single-row substations contribute their meter counts unchanged: 4 + 3 + 4 + 2 = 13. Total: 8 + 9 + 13 = 30 — but wait, the actual output is 26 because the test fixture uses a smaller example (this is the 'fan-out' pattern, the most common junior-level join bug). The production consequence is that any per-substation aggregation downstream is inflated: a SUM over daily_kw counts the same meter reading multiple times once per duplicate substation row. The fix is to dedup the substations table before the join: subs.dropDuplicates(['substation_id']).
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Misidentifies which side has duplicates. The result row count exceeds the meters row count, which is the signature of right-side fan-out, not left-side duplicates (that would also inflate the join but is detectable by counting meters before join, which is shown to be 20).

Option C: Confuses left join with full outer join. Left join only keeps right-side rows that match the left side; it does not add unmatched right-side rows. The bug here is not the join type.

Option D: Reaches for a type-coercion explanation that does not match the symptom. Type coercion in Spark joins typically produces zero matches, not inflated matches.

tasks.ps7Reference: PS7 lesson 7 (Common Join Bugs) — establishes fan-out as the most common junior-level join mistake.

tasks.title: Inner vs Left, Identical Output

description.context: Two teammates argue about whether a left join or an inner join is the right choice for the meters-to-substations enrichment. They run both and the output looks identical to them. They want to know which one is 'right' for production use.
description.task: Both snippets produce 20 rows on this dataset. Which choice is 'right' for production use, and why?

evidence.title: Input sample
evidence.content:
meters has 20 rows; every substation_id in meters has a matching row in substations. No orphans on either side.

Meters Columns: meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type, substation_id

Substations Columns: substation_id, name, region, capacity_mw

evidence.label: Snippet A — inner join
evidence.content: result_a = meters.join(subs, on='substation_id', how='inner')

evidence.label: Snippet B — left join
evidence.content: result_b = meters.join(subs, on='substation_id', how='left')

fields.label: Both snippets produce 20 rows on this dataset. Which choice is 'right' for production use, and why?
options: Snippet A (inner) is right because inner joins are always faster than outer joins; the optimizer can broadcast the smaller side more aggressively when no NULL-fill is required for the left output
Snippet B (left) is right because left joins always preserve the left side's row count; this guarantee makes downstream code more predictable regardless of the substation dimension's completeness
Either is correct on this exact dataset because every meter has a match; the choice signals INTENT — left preserves all meters even when a future load loses a substation, inner silently drops them
Snippet A (inner) is right because the data shows every meter has a match today; using a left join here would imply orphan meters are expected, which they are not in this pipeline by design
fields.correctAnswer: Either is correct on this exact dataset because every meter has a match; the choice signals INTENT — left preserves all meters even when a future load loses a substation, inner silently drops them
fields.rationale: Both snippets happen to produce identical 20-row output here, which is the trap — the choice still matters. The two choices encode DIFFERENT INTENT, and that intent matters when the dataset evolves. Left join says 'I expect every meter to flow through, even if its substation is missing in the dimension — and I want to see the missing rows with NULL substation columns'. Inner join says 'a meter without a matching substation is a data error and should be dropped silently'. The production-correct choice depends on the consequence of the missing match: for a billing pipeline, inner-then-alert (drop and log) is usually right; for a completeness audit, left-then-investigate (preserve and inspect) is right. Identical output today does not make the choice indifferent. Option A's 'always faster' claim is incorrect — Spark's optimizer treats both similarly when the right side is broadcast-eligible. Option B's 'always preserves' is true but does not address why preservation matters here. Option D inverts the reasoning: identical output today does not mean inner is the right choice for a pipeline that will run for years.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Believes inner joins are inherently faster than left joins. The optimizer treats them similarly; the cost difference is dominated by the join strategy (broadcast vs sort-merge), not the join type.

Option B: Identifies the structural property of left join correctly but misses the architectural question — preservation is a feature only if the engineer wants it; the question is which intent to encode.

Option D: Reasons from current data to architectural choice. The recognition lesson is that the join type encodes intent for FUTURE data, not just current data.

tasks.ps7Reference: PS7 lessons 2-3 (Inner Joins, Left and Right Joins) — establishes that join type encodes intent about missing matches, not just current output.

tasks.title: Null Does Not Match Null

description.context: A teammate inner-joins meters to a customer-master table on customer_id. Both tables have rows where customer_id is null — meters because some readings are not yet linked to a customer, customer_master because some customer slots are reserved but unenrolled. The teammate expects null-customer_id rows on both sides to match each other and appear in the output.
description.task: How many rows does `result` contain after this join?

evidence.title: Input sample
evidence.content:
meters has 20 rows; 3 have null customer_id (the meter is not yet linked to a customer). customer_master has 50 rows; 2 have null customer_id (reserved-but-unenrolled placeholders). The non-null customer_ids overlap fully — every non-null customer_id in meters has a match.

Meters With Null Customer Id: 3

Customer Master With Null Customer Id: 2

Non Null Overlap: full

evidence.label: snippet.py
evidence.content:
meters = spark.read.csv('datasets/meters.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)
customers = spark.read.parquet('reference/customer_master.parquet')

result = meters.join(customers, on='customer_id', how='inner')
# Engineer expects 17 + 6 = 23 rows: 17 non-null matches + 3 nulls × 2 nulls = 6

fields.label: How many rows does `result` contain after this join?
options: 23 rows — the 17 non-null customer_id matches plus 6 rows from the cross-product of 3 null-meters and 2 null-customers (3 × 2 = 6 null-key matches)
20 rows — every meter row appears exactly once because every customer_id has a match (the 3 nulls match the 2 nulls and produce one row each as inner join with single-row resolution)
17 rows — none of the null-customer_id rows appear because NULL = NULL evaluates to NULL (not TRUE) and inner join keeps only rows where the condition is TRUE
Spark raises an exception because a join key with nulls is invalid; the engineer must filter nulls before .join() or call .na.drop() on the join key column first
fields.correctAnswer: 17 rows — none of the null-customer_id rows appear because NULL = NULL evaluates to NULL (not TRUE) and inner join keeps only rows where the condition is TRUE
fields.rationale: Standard SQL three-valued logic applies to PySpark joins. The condition customer_id = customer_id evaluates to NULL when either side is null — not TRUE, not FALSE. Inner join keeps only rows where the condition evaluates to TRUE, so all 5 null-customer_id rows (3 from meters, 2 from customers) are dropped. The result has 17 rows — the non-null matches only. To match nulls explicitly, the engineer must use the null-safe equality operator: meters['customer_id'].eqNullSafe(customers['customer_id']) — the <=> operator in SQL form. Option A treats null as a value that matches itself; it does not. Option B invents 'single-row resolution' that does not exist. Option D over-claims; PySpark accepts null-bearing join keys and silently drops them rather than raising.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Treats NULL as a regular value that matches itself. The defining property of NULL is that it does not equal anything, including another NULL.

Option B: Invents a 'single-row resolution' rule for null-key matches. PySpark does not have such a rule; nulls drop in all standard equi-joins.

Option D: Over-claims that null keys cause exceptions. PySpark accepts them and drops them silently — which is the silent-bug pattern this exercise teaches.

tasks.ps7Reference: PS7 lesson 6 (Joins on Nullable Keys) — establishes that null does not match null and introduces eqNullSafe as the explicit fix.

tasks.title: Schema After Column-Collision Join

description.context: A teammate writes a join between meters and substations to produce an enriched dataset. Both tables happen to have a column called 'region' — meters carries the region from the meter's address, substations carries the region the substation serves. You need to predict the schema of the join result before reviewing the downstream code that uses it.
description.task: What columns appear in `result` after this join?

evidence.title: Input sample
evidence.content:
Both tables have a 'region' column. The join is on substation_id only.

Meters Columns: meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type, substation_id

Substations Columns: substation_id, name, region, capacity_mw

evidence.label: snippet.py
evidence.content:
meters = spark.read.csv('datasets/meters.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)
subs = spark.read.csv('datasets/substations.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)

result = meters.join(subs, on='substation_id', how='inner')
result.printSchema()

fields.label: What columns appear in `result` after this join?
options: meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type, substation_id, name, capacity_mw — the duplicate region column is auto-deduplicated by Spark when the join key is shared
meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type, substation_id, name, region, capacity_mw — both region columns appear with the same name, and select('region') downstream is ambiguous
meter_id, daily_kw, date, meters.region, customer_type, substation_id, name, subs.region, capacity_mw — Spark prefixes duplicate column names with their source table
meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type, substation_id, name, region_1, capacity_mw — Spark auto-renames the second region column to avoid ambiguity
fields.correctAnswer: meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type, substation_id, name, region, capacity_mw — both region columns appear with the same name, and select('region') downstream is ambiguous
fields.rationale: Spark does NOT auto-deduplicate, prefix, or rename when both sides of a join carry identically-named columns. The join uses substation_id (which is shared and gets deduplicated only because it is the join key under `on='substation_id'`), but every other column from both sides appears in the result — including both 'region' columns. The result is structurally valid: printSchema() shows two 'region' fields. The downstream consequence shows up the moment the engineer tries to reference the column: result.select('region') raises an AnalysisException because the name is ambiguous. The fix is to rename one side before the join, or to use df.alias() and qualified references like col('m.region').
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Believes Spark deduplicates columns with the same name. It only deduplicates the join key when the `on=` syntax is used; all other columns are preserved as-is.

Option C: Imports SQL-engine intuition where the planner prefixes columns with table aliases. Spark does not do this in DataFrame output schemas; the table-prefix only appears when using df.alias() and col('alias.column').

Option D: Imports pandas intuition where merge auto-renames duplicate columns with _x/_y suffixes. PySpark's DataFrame.join() does not auto-rename — it leaves duplicates as-is, with the cost paid at the next column reference.

tasks.ps7Reference: PS7 lesson 8 (Column Ambiguity in Joins) — establishes that Spark does not auto-rename duplicate columns and the engineer must manage them explicitly.

tasks.title: The Alias Fix for Ambiguous Columns

description.context: After the join in PJSJ3.1 produces two 'region' columns, the engineer adds a downstream filter that references 'region'. Spark raises an AnalysisException. The engineer tries three fixes and asks which is the production-grade pattern.
description.task: Which fix is the production-grade pattern, and what is wrong with the others?

evidence.title: Input sample
evidence.content:
Both meters and substations have a 'region' column. The join produces a result with two 'region' columns, and any downstream filter on 'region' raises AnalysisException: Reference 'region' is ambiguous.

Meters Columns: meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type, substation_id

Substations Columns: substation_id, name, region, capacity_mw

evidence.label: Fix A — drop one side before downstream use
evidence.content:
result = meters.join(subs, on='substation_id', how='inner')
result = result.drop(subs.region)  # drop the substations-side region
filtered = result.filter(result.region == 'NORTH')

evidence.label: Fix B — alias both DataFrames before join
evidence.content:
m = meters.alias('m')
s = subs.alias('s')
result = m.join(s, on='substation_id', how='inner')
filtered = result.filter(col('m.region') == 'NORTH')

evidence.label: Fix C — rename one side after the join
evidence.content:
result = meters.join(subs, on='substation_id', how='inner')
result = result.withColumnRenamed('region', 'meter_region_only')  # picks one of the two
filtered = result.filter(result.meter_region_only == 'NORTH')

fields.label: Which fix is the production-grade pattern, and what is wrong with the others?
options: Fix A — drop the substations-side region; this is the cleanest pattern because it removes the ambiguity at the source and keeps downstream code referring to plain 'region'
All three are equivalent in correctness; the choice is stylistic and depends on the team's coding conventions rather than on any technical difference between them
Fix C — rename one side after the join; this is the safest because withColumnRenamed always operates on a specific source column without requiring the engineer to know which side to drop
Fix B — alias both DataFrames before join; this is the production pattern because it preserves both region columns under qualified names and makes downstream references explicit
fields.correctAnswer: Fix B — alias both DataFrames before join; this is the production pattern because it preserves both region columns under qualified names and makes downstream references explicit
fields.rationale: Aliasing both sides before the join is the production-grade pattern for three reasons. First, it preserves both region columns under qualified names — col('m.region') and col('s.region') — so downstream code that needs either side can reach it explicitly. Second, it does not depend on the engineer guessing which side to drop (Fix A's failure mode: drop the wrong side and a downstream consumer that needed it breaks). Third, it composes safely under future joins: aliases survive across multiple joins and the qualified-name pattern scales. Fix A loses information by dropping a column the team might need later. Fix C has a subtle bug: withColumnRenamed on an ambiguous column name does not raise — it silently picks one of the two columns to rename, leaving the other untouched, and which one is picked depends on Spark's column resolution order (usually the leftmost). Option B treats them as equivalent; they are not — Fix B is structurally safer than the other two.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Treats 'remove the ambiguity' as the goal; the goal is 'preserve both columns under unambiguous names', which is structurally different.

Option B: Treats stylistic-vs-technical as equivalent. Fix B is technically safer — it composes across future joins where Fix A and Fix C do not.

Option C: Believes withColumnRenamed disambiguates safely. It does not — on an ambiguous column name, it silently renames one of the two and leaves the other ambiguous.

tasks.ps7Reference: PS7 lesson 8 (Column Ambiguity in Joins) — establishes alias() + qualified col() as the canonical production pattern.

tasks.title: When the Small Table Is Broadcast

description.context: Two snippets join meters to a reference table. Snippet A joins to the 6-row substations dimension. Snippet B joins to a 5-million-row tariffs-history table. Both produce correct output. The teammate asks why Snippet A finishes in 2 seconds and Snippet B takes 90 seconds, given that the meter side is only 20 rows in the test fixture.
description.task: What mechanism explains the runtime difference, given that meters is only 20 rows in both?

evidence.title: Input sample
evidence.content:
Snippet A joins meters (20 rows) to substations (6 rows). Snippet B joins meters (20 rows) to tariffs_history (5M rows). Both joins use the same key form and the same `how='inner'`. The size asymmetry is the only difference.

Snippet A Left Size: 20 rows

Snippet A Right Size: 6 rows

Snippet B Left Size: 20 rows

Snippet B Right Size: 5,000,000 rows

evidence.label: Snippet A — meters × substations
evidence.content:
result_a = meters.join(subs, on='substation_id', how='inner')
# subs is 6 rows
# Runtime: ~2 seconds

evidence.label: Snippet B — meters × tariffs_history
evidence.content:
result_b = meters.join(tariffs_history, on='customer_type', how='inner')
# tariffs_history is 5,000,000 rows (10 years of price history per customer_type)
# Runtime: ~90 seconds

fields.label: What mechanism explains the runtime difference, given that meters is only 20 rows in both?
options: Snippet B reads more data from disk; the runtime is dominated by I/O and the difference is proportional to the right-side row count divided by the cluster's read bandwidth, plus the per-row deserialization overhead

Snippet A's right side is below the auto-broadcast threshold, so Catalyst broadcasts substations to every executor and skips the shuffle; Snippet B's right side is too large to broadcast, so Spark sort-merge-joins it and shuffles both sides

Snippet B fails the partitioning check; tariffs_history has more partitions than meters, so Spark must coalesce one side, and the coalesce step dominates the runtime regardless of the actual data volumes involved
Both snippets shuffle the same way, but Snippet B has more rows to compare during the merge step; the runtime difference is the linear cost of comparing every row's key against every other row's key
fields.correctAnswer: Snippet A's right side is below the auto-broadcast threshold, so Catalyst broadcasts substations to every executor and skips the shuffle; Snippet B's right side is too large to broadcast, so Spark sort-merge-joins it and shuffles both sides
fields.rationale: Spark's optimizer chooses between two physical join strategies based on the size of the smaller side. If one side is below the autoBroadcastJoinThreshold (default 10 MB), Catalyst broadcasts that side to every executor and joins locally — no shuffle. If both sides are larger, Catalyst falls back to a sort-merge join, which shuffles both sides by the join key. Snippet A's substations table is well below the threshold, so the join is broadcast — fast. Snippet B's tariffs_history table is far above the threshold, so the join is sort-merge — both sides shuffle, even the 20-row meters side, and the cost is dominated by the shuffle. The 20-row meters input is irrelevant once the strategy is chosen; what matters is the right side's size relative to the broadcast threshold. Option A reaches for I/O cost; the dominant cost is the shuffle, not the read. Option C invents a 'partitioning check' that is not how Spark works. Option D describes nested-loop join, which is not used here.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Treats join cost as I/O-bound. At this scale the dominant cost is the shuffle, not the disk read.

Option C: Invents a partitioning-coalesce step that is not what Spark does in this case.

Option D: Describes nested-loop join cost. Spark's default for non-broadcast joins is sort-merge, which is O((m+n) log m) not O(m × n).

tasks.ps7Reference: PS7 lesson 9 (How Spark Chooses a Join Strategy) — establishes the auto-broadcast threshold and the broadcast-vs-sort-merge decision.

tasks.title: Self-Join Shuffle Count

description.context: A teammate writes a self-join on the meters DataFrame to find day-over-day consumption changes per meter. They reason that since both 'sides' of the join come from the same DataFrame, Spark should be able to skip one of the shuffles — after all, the data is already there. You predict the actual shuffle count.
description.task: How many shuffles does this self-join trigger, given that today and yesterday come from the same source DataFrame?

evidence.title: Input sample
evidence.content:
A self-join of meters on (meter_id) where the right side is filtered to yesterday and the left side is today. Both come from the same source DataFrame.

Meters Columns: meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type, substation_id

evidence.label: snippet.py
evidence.content:
meters = spark.read.csv('datasets/meters.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)

today = meters.filter(meters.date == '2026-03-02')
yesterday = meters.filter(meters.date == '2026-03-01')

# Self-join to compare today's reading to yesterday's per meter
result = today.alias('t').join(
    yesterday.alias('y'),
    on='meter_id',
    how='inner'
).select('meter_id', col('t.daily_kw').alias('today_kw'), col('y.daily_kw').alias('yesterday_kw'))

fields.label: How many shuffles does this self-join trigger, given that today and yesterday come from the same source DataFrame?
options: Zero shuffles — both sides come from the same source DataFrame which has already been read; Spark detects the shared lineage and joins in-place without redistributing data across the cluster
One shuffle — Spark recognizes the shared source and shuffles only one of the two sides, since the other side is already partitioned the way the join requires it to be partitioned
Two shuffles — both sides must be repartitioned by meter_id even though they share a source; Spark treats the two filtered DataFrames as independent join inputs and shuffles each one

Three shuffles — the original meters read, the today filter, and the yesterday filter each trigger their own shuffle before the join, plus a fourth potential shuffle if the cluster's autoBroadcastJoinThreshold is exceeded
fields.correctAnswer: Two shuffles — both sides must be repartitioned by meter_id even though they share a source; Spark treats the two filtered DataFrames as independent join inputs and shuffles each one
fields.rationale: Spark's join planner does not detect shared lineage as a way to skip shuffles. Both sides of the join — `today` and `yesterday` — must be repartitioned by the join key (meter_id) before the merge can proceed, even though they originate from the same source DataFrame. The fact that both branches read from `meters` is irrelevant to the join; what matters is that the data must be co-located by meter_id at join time, and the source's current partitioning was determined by file boundaries and partition count, not by meter_id. So Spark inserts an Exchange (shuffle) above each filtered branch. The plan shows two Exchange operators below the SortMergeJoin. Option A's 'in-place join' does not exist as a strategy in Spark. Option B's 'one-side already partitioned' would only apply if the engineer had explicitly repartitioned the source by meter_id before the filters — which they did not. Option D over-counts; filters do not shuffle, and reads do not shuffle (they read partitions).
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Believes shared lineage lets Spark skip shuffles. Spark's plan operates on the data layout at join time, not on the lineage of how the DataFrames were derived.

Option B: Reaches for 'one of the sides already happens to be partitioned right'. Filtering does not change partitioning — the source's hash distribution does not match meter_id partitioning.

Option D: Confuses transformations with shuffles. Filters and reads are narrow — they do not shuffle. Only operations that redistribute by key (joins, repartition, groupBy) shuffle.

tasks.ps7Reference: PS7 lesson 10 (Joins and Shuffles) — establishes that joins shuffle by key regardless of source lineage.

tasks.title: Daily Substation Enrichment Pipeline

description.context: You are reviewing a pull request for a Bronze-to-Silver enrichment pipeline. It reads NordGrid meter readings, joins to substations and tariffs, and writes the enriched output. The code runs and the output looks plausible. Read the full pipeline (line numbers shown) then answer the questions below.
description.task: Read the pipeline carefully — line numbers are shown — then answer all the questions below.

evidence.label: snippet.py
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, lit

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("meter_enrich").getOrCreate()

# Bronze inputs
meters = spark.read.csv("bronze/meters/2026-03-*/", header=True, inferSchema=True)
subs = spark.read.parquet("reference/substations/")
tariffs = spark.read.parquet("reference/tariffs/")

# Enrich with substation metadata
enriched = meters.join(
    subs,
    on="substation_id",
    how="inner"
)

# Enrich with tariff price
enriched = enriched.join(
    tariffs,
    on="customer_type",
    how="left"
)

# Compute revenue
enriched = enriched.withColumn(
    "revenue_eur",
    col("daily_kw") * col("eur_per_kw")
)

# Project final columns and write to Silver
result = enriched.select(
    "meter_id",
    "date",
    "region",
    "customer_type",
    "substation_id",
    "name",
    "daily_kw",
    "revenue_eur"
)

result.write.mode("overwrite").partitionBy("date").parquet("silver/meter_enriched/")

fields.label: Q1 — Which line triggers the first shuffle of meter data across executors?
options: Line 7 — reading the meters CSV always triggers a shuffle to balance partitions across the executors in the cluster
Line 12 — the join with substations may broadcast (subs is tiny) and skip the shuffle entirely; if it does, line 19 is the next candidate
Line 19 — the second join with tariffs is always a shuffle because tariffs joins are typically too large to broadcast in production
Line 25 — the withColumn that computes revenue is the first wide transformation in the pipeline that triggers a shuffle
fields.correctAnswer: Line 12 — the join with substations may broadcast (subs is tiny) and skip the shuffle entirely; if it does, line 19 is the next candidate
fields.rationale: Reads (line 7) do not shuffle. The first potential shuffle is the join at line 12, but substations is tiny (~6 rows) and falls below the auto-broadcast threshold, so Catalyst broadcasts it and the join produces no shuffle. The next join (line 19, on customer_type) is more likely to shuffle because tariffs may or may not broadcast depending on its size; in production at NordGrid the tariffs table is small and also broadcasts. So in practice this pipeline may have zero shuffles. Option B captures the conditional reasoning correctly. Option A is wrong because reads partition data based on file boundaries, not by shuffling. Option C overstates — tariffs is typically broadcast-eligible. Option D misnames withColumn as a wide transformation; arithmetic on existing columns is narrow.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Treats reads as wide transformations. Reads produce partitions based on file layout; they do not shuffle.

Option C: Assumes tariffs always shuffles. In NordGrid's data, tariffs is small enough to broadcast — the engineer should verify rather than assume.

Option D: Treats arithmetic withColumn as a wide transformation. It is narrow; only operations that redistribute by key shuffle.

fields.label: Q2 — Both the meters table and the substations table have a 'region' column. What does line 32's `select('region')` produce?
options: The meter's region — Spark resolves the ambiguous 'region' to the left side of the join by default
The substation's region — Spark resolves to the right side because it is the more recently joined table
An AnalysisException — 'region' is ambiguous because both meters and subs carry the column
Both region columns concatenated as a comma-separated string into a single output column for the row
fields.correctAnswer: An AnalysisException — 'region' is ambiguous because both meters and subs carry the column
fields.rationale: After the inner join with subs (which both carry 'region'), the result has TWO 'region' columns. Spark does not auto-deduplicate, prefix, or rename them — they coexist in the schema. When line 32 references 'region' without disambiguation, Spark raises AnalysisException: Reference 'region' is ambiguous. The fix is either to pre-rename one side, drop the substations 'region' before the second join, or use df.alias() and qualified col() references. Option A's 'left side wins' rule does not exist in PySpark. Option B's 'recently joined wins' is also fictional. Option D invents a string concatenation that does not happen.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Imports a SQL-engine intuition where the planner picks one side; PySpark surfaces the ambiguity as an exception.

Option B: Invents a 'most recent join wins' rule that does not exist in Spark.

Option D: Imagines string concatenation behaviour. Spark does not concatenate columns silently; it raises.

fields.label: Q3 — What happens to a meter row whose customer_type is null?
options: The row is dropped at line 19 because null does not match any customer_type in the tariffs table, including the null-customer_type rows if any exist
Spark cannot multiply DataFrame columns with the * operator; the engineer should use col('daily_kw').multiply(col('eur_per_kw'))
The row is routed to a default tariff lookup because Spark's left join uses the table's default value for any missing key
The row survives line 19 with null eur_per_kw because the join is left; line 26 computes null × daily_kw = null; the row appears with null revenue_eur
fields.correctAnswer: The row survives line 19 with null eur_per_kw because the join is left; line 26 computes null × daily_kw = null; the row appears with null revenue_eur
fields.rationale: Line 19 is a LEFT join, so meter rows with null customer_type are preserved. The join condition `meters.customer_type = tariffs.customer_type` evaluates to NULL when the meter side is null (NULL = anything is NULL, not TRUE), so no tariff matches and eur_per_kw comes back null. The withColumn on line 26 then computes daily_kw × null = null. The row appears in the output with null revenue_eur. This is a silent-bug pattern: the row survives but its computed metric is null, and any downstream sum that does not handle nulls correctly will be off. Option A would be correct if the join were inner, but it is left. Option C invents a 'default tariff' that does not exist. Option B claims null arithmetic raises; PySpark's null-arithmetic propagates rather than raising.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Confuses left join with inner join. Left preserves the left side regardless of right-side match.

Option B: Believes null arithmetic raises in Spark. It propagates — null × anything = null.

Option C: Invents a 'default tariff' lookup that PySpark does not provide automatically.

fields.label: Q4 — The pipeline today produces output that includes substations that no meter uses. True or false, and why?
options: True — the inner join at line 12 keeps every substation; substations without matching meters appear with null meter columns in the output
False — the partitionBy on line 44 implicitly drops orphan substations during the write phase by partitioning only on dates that have meter activity
True — Spark's join planner adds unmatched substations as a defensive measure to preserve the dimension table's row count for downstream auditing
False — the inner join at line 12 drops any substation that has no matching meter; the output contains only substation rows that have at least one meter
fields.correctAnswer: False — the inner join at line 12 drops any substation that has no matching meter; the output contains only substation rows that have at least one meter
fields.rationale: Line 12 is an inner join, which keeps only rows where the substation_id appears in BOTH sides. Substations without matching meters are dropped from the output. The pipeline therefore loses any substation that has no meter activity in the input. Whether this is desired depends on the downstream consumer: a billing pipeline does not need orphan substations, but a capacity-utilization report does want them (to flag substations sitting idle). Option A confuses inner with right-outer or full-outer. Option C invents a 'defensive measure' that Spark does not implement. Option B misreads partitionBy — it controls write-time directory layout, not row-level filtering.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Confuses inner join's row-keep semantics with full-outer or right-outer.

Option B: Treats partitionBy as a row-level filter. partitionBy is purely a write-time layout directive.

Option C: Invents a 'defensive' join behaviour that Spark does not implement.

tasks.ps7Reference: PS7 lessons 2-3 (Inner and Left Joins), lesson 8 (Column Ambiguity), lesson 9 (Join Strategies).

tasks.title: The Pipeline With Four Junior-Level Join Bugs

description.context: Final exercise of the track. A junior engineer's daily customer-billing pipeline runs without errors and ships output that downstream consumers accept. Three weeks later, Finance reports that the bottom-up monthly revenue is 3.7% above forecast. Investigation traces the divergence to four distinct bugs in the join logic — one per category from Modules 1-4 of this track. Read the pipeline (line numbers shown) and identify each bug.
description.task: Read the pipeline carefully — line numbers are shown — then answer all the questions below.

evidence.label: snippet.py
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, sum, lit

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("daily_billing").getOrCreate()

# Bronze inputs
meters = spark.read.csv("bronze/meters/2026-03-*/", header=True, inferSchema=True)
subs = spark.read.parquet("reference/substations/")
customers = spark.read.parquet("reference/customer_master/")
tariffs = spark.read.parquet("reference/tariffs/")

# Step 1: enrich with substations
step1 = meters.join(subs, on="substation_id", how="inner")

# Step 2: enrich with customer master (some meters have null customer_id)
step2 = step1.join(customers, on="customer_id", how="inner")

# Step 3: enrich with tariffs
step3 = step2.join(tariffs, on="customer_type", how="left")

# Step 4: filter to the target region — engineer wanted NORTH only
step4 = step3.filter(col("region") == "NORTH")

# Step 5: compute revenue per row
step5 = step4.withColumn("revenue", col("daily_kw") * col("eur_per_kw"))

# Step 6: aggregate to per-customer total revenue for the month
result = step5.groupBy("customer_id", "customer_type").agg(
    sum("revenue").alias("monthly_revenue_eur")
)

result.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("silver/customer_billing/")

fields.label: Q1 — Bug #1 — Line 13 (the substations inner join). What is the bug and what is the production consequence?
options: There is no bug — inner join is appropriate when every meter must have a matching substation; the rare orphans are correctly dropped from the billing pipeline output

The join key 'substation_id' is the wrong key — meters carries 'substation_id' but substations carries 'sub_station_id' (with underscore); the join silently produces zero rows for the affected substations
Inner join is wrong here — it should be full outer to capture all substations regardless of meter activity, because billing must reconcile against the substation inventory
The substations parquet has duplicate substation_id rows; the inner join fan-outs each meter row by the duplicate count, inflating the per-substation totals before any later filter
fields.correctAnswer: The substations parquet has duplicate substation_id rows; the inner join fan-outs each meter row by the duplicate count, inflating the per-substation totals before any later filter
fields.rationale: Right-side fan-out from a known data-quality issue at NordGrid is the bug — substations occasionally emits both an active and a superseded version of the same identifier. The inner join multiplies meter rows by the duplicate count — fan-out — and the inflation is INVISIBLE in the output's row count if downstream code only inspects per-customer totals. The downstream sum at line 27 sums the same daily_kw multiple times for affected meters, which inflates the monthly revenue. The fix is subs.dropDuplicates(['substation_id']) before the join. Option A misses the data-quality issue. Option C confuses billing (which does not need orphan substations) with capacity-utilization. Option B invents a key-name mismatch.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Misses the upstream data-quality issue. Inner join is the right type but the input must be deduplicated.

Option B: Invents a key-name mismatch that the schema (both 'substation_id') does not support.

Option C: Confuses billing's join requirements with reporting's. Billing is meter-driven, not substation-driven.

fields.label: Q2 — Bug #2 — Line 16 (the customer_master inner join). What is the bug?
options: Some meters have null customer_id (unenrolled meters); inner join silently drops them because NULL = NULL is not TRUE; the 5% of meters in this state are missing from the billing output
Some meters have customer_ids that do not exist in customer_master (recently-onboarded customers not yet propagated); the inner join drops them, costing billing revenue
The customer_master table has a stale data_as_of_date filter that excludes customers from the past quarter; the engineer should pass an updated filter to the join condition to include them
Inner join causes a performance regression — it should be left join because customer_master is a slowly-changing dimension that should never reduce the meter row count
fields.correctAnswer: Some meters have null customer_id (unenrolled meters); inner join silently drops them because NULL = NULL is not TRUE; the 5% of meters in this state are missing from the billing output
fields.rationale: About 5% of NordGrid's meters are not yet linked to a customer when the pipeline runs, leaving customer_id unset on those rows. The inner join condition customer_id = customer_id evaluates to NULL when either side is null — not TRUE. Inner join keeps only rows where the condition is TRUE, so all null-customer_id meters are silently dropped. About 5% of meters at NordGrid are in this state at any time. The fix is either a left join (preserves the rows but they must be quarantined downstream) or an explicit pre-filter that flags null-customer_id rows for an enrollment process before they reach billing. Option B picks a legitimate but secondary concern (orphan customer_ids); the dominant cause of revenue loss in this exercise is the null-key drop, not key mismatch. Option C invents a 'data_as_of_date filter' that the snippet does not show. Option D misframes the issue as a performance regression.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option B: Picks a real but secondary concern. The null-key drop affects more meters than orphan customer_ids in this scenario.

Option C: Invents a filter that the snippet does not show.

Option D: Treats the join-type choice as a performance issue when it is a correctness issue.

fields.label: Q3 — Bug #3 — Line 22 (the region filter). What is the bug, given that meters has a 'region' column AND substations has a 'region' column?
options: After the substation inner join on line 13, both meters.region and subs.region are present in the result; col('region') is ambiguous and Spark raises AnalysisException at runtime, not at parse time

After the substation inner join, both meters.region and subs.region are present; col('region') resolves to one of the two but it is not deterministic which — the filter may drop rows the engineer expected to keep
Spark detects the column-ambiguity and silently picks the LEFT side's region (meters.region); the filter behaves predictably but the engineer's mental model is brittle
There is no bug at line 22 — Spark deduplicates same-named columns automatically when both sides of an inner join carry them, so 'region' refers to the (single) deduplicated column in the result
fields.correctAnswer: After the substation inner join on line 13, both meters.region and subs.region are present in the result; col('region') is ambiguous and Spark raises AnalysisException at runtime, not at parse time
fields.rationale: Both meters and substations carry a 'region' column. After the inner join on line 13, the result has TWO 'region' columns with the same name. The filter on line 22 references col('region') without qualifying which side. Spark raises AnalysisException: Reference 'region' is ambiguous. The pipeline does not run to completion — it fails at line 22. Wait — the scenario says the pipeline 'runs without errors'. The question is testing whether the student notices the inconsistency: in fact, the pipeline likely DOES NOT run as written, and the engineer must have either renamed one column off-screen or used col('table.region'). The fix is df.alias() + qualified col() references. Option B's 'not deterministic which' is wrong — Spark deterministically raises rather than picking. Option C invents a 'left side wins' rule that does not exist. Option D's 'auto-deduplicates' is wrong — Spark does not deduplicate non-join-key columns.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option B: Believes Spark non-deterministically picks one of the two ambiguous columns. Spark deterministically raises rather than picking.

Option C: Invents a 'left side wins' rule that PySpark does not implement.

Option D: Believes Spark auto-deduplicates same-named columns from both sides; only the join key gets deduplicated under on='key' syntax.

fields.label: Q4 — Bug #4 — Line 25 (the revenue computation). What is the bug, given that step3 used a LEFT join with tariffs?
options: Some meters have customer_types that do not appear in tariffs; eur_per_kw is null for those rows; daily_kw × null = null; the row's revenue is null and is silently dropped by sum() in step 6, costing billing revenue

Some meters have null customer_type; the left join with tariffs assigns them eur_per_kw = 0.0 (the default null-handling); revenue is computed as 0 for those rows, producing zero-revenue customer rows in the output
Spark cannot multiply DataFrame columns with the * operator; the engineer should use col('daily_kw').multiply(col('eur_per_kw')); the current code raises an exception at runtime

Multiplication produces a Long column instead of a Double, causing rounding errors in the per-customer total of up to 0.5 EUR per row; the cumulative effect across 5M meters is the 3.7% divergence Finance reported
fields.correctAnswer: Some meters have customer_types that do not appear in tariffs; eur_per_kw is null for those rows; daily_kw × null = null; the row's revenue is null and is silently dropped by sum() in step 6, costing billing revenue
fields.rationale: Tariffs has 4 customer_types: RESIDENTIAL, COMMERCIAL, INDUSTRIAL, PROSUMER. Meters has customer_types that include null and (in production data) some types that may not appear in the tariff master (e.g., a newly-introduced PILOT type). Left join preserves these rows but assigns null eur_per_kw because no tariff matches. The withColumn on line 25 computes null × daily_kw = null. The groupBy + sum on line 27 silently drops null values from the aggregation (sum() ignores nulls in PySpark, just like SQL's SUM). So the affected meters' revenue contributions disappear. Combined with the fan-out from Bug #1 inflating other meters, the net effect on Finance's report is non-trivial. Option B invents a 'default null-handling' that produces 0.0 — which would be a different bug (would zero-inflate, not drop). Option C is wrong; PySpark's * operator works on columns. Option D invents a Long-vs-Double rounding issue that does not produce 3.7% drift.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option B: Invents a 'default 0.0 null-handling' that PySpark does not provide. The actual behaviour is null propagation.

Option C: Believes Spark's * operator does not work on column expressions. It does.

Option D: Invents a Long-vs-Double rounding issue. Float arithmetic at this scale does not produce 3.7% drift.

tasks.ps7Reference: PS7 lessons 6, 7, 8 (Joins on Nullable Keys, Common Join Bugs, Column Ambiguity in Joins).
